certificate.moduleName: Dell BSAFE™ Crypto Module for C
certificate.caveat: Interim validation. When operated in approved mode. When installed, initialized and configured as specified in Section 11 of the Security Policy
certificate.description: Dell BSAFE™ Crypto Module for C software library is designed for developers to incorporate FIPS 140-3 approved cryptography into C-based products. BSAFE™ Crypto Module security software helps protect sensitive data as it is stored, using strong encryption techniques that ease integration with existing data models. Using the capabilities of BSAFE™ Crypto Module software in applications helps provide a persistent level of protection for data, lessening the risk of internal, as well as external, compromise.

acronyms.definition: Advanced Encryption Standard. A fast symmetric key algorithm with a 128-bit block, and keys of lengths 128, 192, and 256 bits. AES replaces DES as the US symmetric encryption standard.
acronyms.name: AES

acronyms.definition: Application Programming Interface.
acronyms.name: API

acronyms.definition: An attempt, either a successful or unsuccessful, to break part or all of a cryptosystem. Attack types include an algebraic attack, birthday attack, brute force attack, chosen ciphertext attack, chosen plaintext attack, differential cryptanalysis, known plaintext attack, linear cryptanalysis, and middle person attack.
acronyms.name: Attack

acronyms.definition: Cryptographic Algorithm Self-Tests. A test of all cryptographic functions of an approved cryptographic algorithm that must be performed prior to the first operational use of the cryptographic algorithm. A CAST may be a KAT, a comparison test or a fault-detection test.
acronyms.name: CAST

acronyms.definition: Cipher Block Chaining. A mode of encryption in which each ciphertext depends upon all previous ciphertexts. Changing the IV alters the ciphertext produced by successive encryptions of an identical plaintext.
acronyms.name: CBC

acronyms.definition: Counter with Cipher block chaining Message authentication code. A mode of encryption combining the Counter mode of encryption with Cipher Block Chaining Message Authentication Code (CBC-MAC) for authentication.
acronyms.name: CCM

acronyms.definition: Cipher Feedback. A mode of encryption producing a stream of ciphertext bits rather than a succession of blocks. In other respects, it has similar properties to the CBC mode of operation.
acronyms.name: CFB

acronyms.definition: Cipher-based Message Authentication Code. An algorithm for calculating a MAC using a block cipher and a secret key, providing verification of both message integrity and authenticity.
acronyms.name: CMAC

acronyms.definition: Cryptographic Module Validation Program.
acronyms.name: CMVP

acronyms.definition: Counter Mode. A mode of encryption which turns a block cipher into a stream cipher. It generates the next keystream block by encrypting successive values of a counter.
acronyms.name: CTR

acronyms.definition: Counter mode Deterministic Random Bit Generator.
acronyms.name: CTR DRBG

acronyms.definition: The conversion of encrypted data, ciphertext, into its original form. Generally, the reverse of encryption.
acronyms.name: Decryption

acronyms.definition: Data Encryption Standard. A symmetric encryption algorithm with a 56-bit key with eight parity bits.
acronyms.name: DES

acronyms.definition: Triple Data Encryption Standard. A symmetric encryption algorithm with three 56-bit keys, with eight parity bits. Also known as Triple-DES and TDEA.
acronyms.name: DES3

acronyms.definition: Diffie-Hellman. An asymmetric key exchange algorithm. There are many variants, but typically two entities exchange some public information (for example, public keys or random values) and combine them with their own private keys to generate a shared session key. As private keys are not transmitted, eavesdroppers are not privy to all of the information comprising the session key.
acronyms.name: DH

acronyms.definition: Deterministic Random Bit Generator.
acronyms.name: DRBG

acronyms.definition: Digital Signature Algorithm. An algorithm for creating digital signatures.
acronyms.name: DSA

acronyms.definition: Elliptic Curve
acronyms.name: EC

acronyms.definition: Elliptic Curve Cryptography. A public-key cryptographic approach based on the algebraic structure of elliptic curves over finite fields. It has the advantage of allowing smaller keys to provide equivalent security.
acronyms.name: ECC

acronyms.definition: Electronic Codebook. A mode of encryption which divides a message into blocks and encrypts each block separately.
acronyms.name: ECB

acronyms.definition: Elliptic Curve Diffie-Hellman. A key agreement protocol that provides the means for two parties, each with an elliptic-curve public/private key pair, to establish a shared secret over an insecure channel.
acronyms.name: ECDH

acronyms.definition: Elliptic Curve Diffie-Hellman with Cofactor key agreement algorithm. This algorithm employs the CDH primitive.
acronyms.name: ECDHC

acronyms.definition: Elliptic Curve Digital Signature Algorithm. A variant of DSA that uses elliptic curve cryptography.
acronyms.name: ECDSA

acronyms.definition: The transformation of plaintext into an apparently less readable form, called ciphertext, using a mathematical process. The ciphertext can be read by anyone who has the key and decrypts (undoes the encryption) the ciphertext.
acronyms.name: Encryption

acronyms.definition: Finite Field Cryptography. The public-key cryptographic methods using operations in a multiplicative group of a finite field. See SP 800-56A Rev. 3. Both DH and DSA are based on Finite Field Cryptography.
acronyms.name: FFC

acronyms.definition: Federal Information Processing Standards.
acronyms.name: FIPS

acronyms.definition: Galois/Counter Mode. A mode of encryption combining the Counter mode of encryption with Galois field multiplication for authentication.
acronyms.name: GCM

acronyms.definition: Galois Message Authentication Mode. An authentication-only variant of GCM.
acronyms.name: GMAC

acronyms.definition: HMAC-based Extract-and Expand KDF. HKDF is a two-step key derivation function, where the first step, extraction, transforms a shared secret into a key-derivation key. The second step, expansion, uses the key-derivation key to derive an output key.
acronyms.name: HKDF

acronyms.definition: Keyed-Hashing for Message Authentication Code.
acronyms.name: HMAC

acronyms.definition: HMAC Deterministic Random Bit Generator.
acronyms.name: HMAC DRBG

acronyms.definition: Initialization Vector. Used as a seed value for an encryption operation.
acronyms.name: IV

acronyms.definition: Known Answer Test.
acronyms.name: KAT

acronyms.definition: Key-Based KDF.
acronyms.name: KBKDF

acronyms.definition: Key Derivation Functions. Deterministic algorithms used to derive cryptographic keying material from a secret value such as a password.
acronyms.name: KDF

acronyms.definition: A method of encrypting key data for protection on untrusted storage devices or during transmission over an insecure channel.
acronyms.name: Key wrapping

acronyms.definition: Message Authentication Code. Apiece of information is attached to a message for verification of the messages authenticity and data integrity.
acronyms.name: MAC

acronyms.definition: A message digest algorithm, which hashes an arbitrary-length input into a 16-byte digest. Designed as a replacement for MD4.
acronyms.name: MD5

acronyms.definition: Non-Deterministic Random Bit Generator.
acronyms.name: NDRBG

acronyms.definition: National Institute of Standards and Technology. A division of the US Department of Commerce (formerly known as the NBS) which produces security and cryptography-related standards.
acronyms.name: NIST

acronyms.definition: Optimal Asymmetric Encryption Padding. A padding scheme often used with RSA encryption to convert the deterministic RSA encryption scheme into a probabilistic scheme.
acronyms.name: OAEP

acronyms.definition: Output Feedback. A mode of encryption in which the cipher is decoupled from its ciphertext.
acronyms.name: OFB

acronyms.definition: Operating System.
acronyms.name: OS

acronyms.definition: A function that uses the HMAC-HASH as the core function in its construction. Specified in RFC 2246 and RFC 5246.
acronyms.name: P_HASH

acronyms.definition: Password-based Key Derivation Function 2. A method of password-based key derivation, which applies a MAC algorithm to derive the key.
acronyms.name: PBKDF2

acronyms.definition: Public Key Cryptography Standards. A group of public-key cryptography standards devised and published by RSA®.
acronyms.name: PKCS

acronyms.definition: Pre-Operational Self-Tests.
acronyms.name: POST

acronyms.definition: Pseudo Random Function. A function that can be used to generate output from a random seed and a data variable such that the output is computationally indistinguishable from truly random output.
acronyms.name: PRF

acronyms.definition: The state or quality of being secluded from the view or presence of others.
acronyms.name: privacy

acronyms.definition: The secret key in public key cryptography. Primarily used for decryption but also used for encryption with digital signatures.
acronyms.name: private key

acronyms.definition: Pseudo-Random Number Generator.
acronyms.name: PRNG

acronyms.definition: Public Security Parameters. Security related public information, for example, public keys, whose modification can compromise the security of the cryptographic module.
acronyms.name: PSP

acronyms.definition: Probabilistic Signature Scheme. A cryptographic signature scheme typically used with RSA signatures.
acronyms.name: PSS

acronyms.definition: Random Number Generator.
acronyms.name: RNG

acronyms.definition: Public key, asymmetric, algorithm providing the ability to encrypt data and create and verify digital signatures. RSA stands for Rivest, Shamir, and Adleman, the developers of the RSA public key cryptosystem.
acronyms.name: RSA

acronyms.definition: Secure Hash Algorithm. An algorithm which creates a unique hash value for each possible input. SHA takes an arbitrary input which is hashed into a 160-bit digest.
acronyms.name: SHA

acronyms.definition: A revision to SHA to correct a weakness. It produces 160-bit digests. SHA-1 takes an arbitrary input, which is hashed into a 20-byte digest.
acronyms.name: SHA-1

acronyms.definition: The NIST-mandated successor to SHA-1, to complement the Advanced Encryption Standard. It is a family of message digest algorithms (SHA2-224, SHA2-256, SHA2-384, SHA2-512, SHA2-512/224, and SHA2-512/256), which produce digests of 224, 256, 384, 512, 224, and 256 bits respectively.
acronyms.name: SHA-2

acronyms.definition: A family of hash algorithms which includes SHA3-224,SHA3-256, SHA3-384 and SHA3-512 bits. It is an alternative to SHA-2,as no significant attacks on SHA-2 are currently known.
acronyms.name: SHA-3

acronyms.definition: An extendable output function (XOF) that produces a variable digest size.
acronyms.name: SHAKE

acronyms.definition: Secure Shell Key Derivation Function. Used by the SSH protocol to derive IVs, encryption keys and integrity keys.
acronyms.name: SSH-KDF

acronyms.definition: Transport Layer Security.
acronyms.name: TLS

acronyms.definition: See DES3.
acronyms.name: Triple-DES

acronyms.definition: XEX-based Tweaked Codebook mode with ciphertext stealing. A mode of encryption used with AES.
acronyms.name: XTS

Sizes approved in FIPS 186-4 and FIPS140-3IG. (CAVP validated)
May be used for verification only.
Advanced Encryption Standard. A fast symmetric key algorithm with a 128-bit block, and keys of lengths 128, 192, and 256 bits. AES replaces DES as the US symmetric encryption standard.

An attempt, either a successful or unsuccessful, to break part or all of a cryptosystem. Attack types include an algebraic attack, birthday attack, brute force attack, chosen ciphertext attack, chosen plaintext attack, differential cryptanalysis, known plaintext attack, linear cryptanalysis, and middle person attack.

Cryptographic Algorithm Self-Tests. A test of all cryptographic functions of an approved cryptographic algorithm that must be performed prior to the first operational use of the cryptographic algorithm. A CAST may be a KAT, a comparison test or a fault-detection test.

Cipher Block Chaining. A mode of encryption in which each ciphertext depends upon all previous ciphertexts. Changing the IV alters the ciphertext produced by successive encryptions of an identical plaintext.

Counter with Cipher block chaining Message authentication code. A mode of encryption combining the Counter mode of encryption with Cipher Block Chaining Message Authentication Code (CBC-MAC) for authentication.
Cipher Feedback. A mode of encryption producing a stream of ciphertext bits rather than a succession of blocks. In other respects, it has similar properties to the CBC mode of operation.
Cipher-based Message Authentication Code. An algorithm for calculating a MAC using a block cipher and a secret key, providing verification of both message integrity and authenticity.
Critical Security Parameters. Security related information, such as keys or passwords, whose disclosure or modification can compromise security.
Counter Mode. A mode of encryption which turns a block cipher into a stream cipher. It generates the next keystream block by encrypting successive values of a counter.
Counter mode Deterministic Random Bit Generator.
The conversion of encrypted data, ciphertext, into its original form. Generally, the reverse of encryption.
Data Encryption Standard. A symmetric encryption algorithm with a 56-bit key with eight parity bits.
Triple Data Encryption Standard. A symmetric encryption algorithm with three 56-bit keys, with eight parity bits. Also known as Triple-DES and TDEA.

Diffie-Hellman. An asymmetric key exchange algorithm. There are many variants, but typically two entities exchange some public information (for example, public keys or random values) and combine them with their own private keys to generate a shared session key. As private keys are not transmitted, eavesdroppers are not privy to all of the information comprising the session key.
Digital Signature Algorithm. An algorithm for creating digital signatures.

Elliptic Curve Cryptography. A public-key cryptographic approach based on the algebraic structure of elliptic curves over finite fields. It has the advantage of allowing smaller keys to provide equivalent security.
Electronic Codebook. A mode of encryption which divides a message into blocks and encrypts each block separately.

Elliptic Curve Diffie-Hellman. A key agreement protocol that provides the means for two parties, each with an elliptic-curve public/private key pair, to establish a shared secret over an insecure channel.
Elliptic Curve Diffie-Hellman with Cofactor key agreement algorithm. This algorithm employs the CDH primitive.
Elliptic Curve Digital Signature Algorithm. A variant of DSA that uses elliptic curve cryptography.

The transformation of plaintext into an apparently less readable form, called ciphertext, using a mathematical process. The ciphertext can be read by anyone who has the key and decrypts (undoes the encryption) the ciphertext.

Finite Field Cryptography. The public-key cryptographic methods using operations in a multiplicative group of a finite field. See SP 800-56A Rev. 3. Both DH and DSA are based on Finite Field Cryptography.
Galois/Counter Mode. A mode of encryption combining the Counter mode of encryption with Galois field multiplication for authentication.
Galois Message Authentication Mode. An authentication-only variant of GCM.

HMAC-based Extract-and Expand KDF. HKDF is a two-step key derivation function, where the first step, extraction, transforms a shared secret into a key-derivation key. The second step, expansion, uses the key-derivation key to derive an output key.
Initialization Vector. Used as a seed value for an encryption operation.
Key Derivation Functions. Deterministic algorithms used to derive cryptographic keying material from a secret value such as a password.

A string of bits used in cryptography, allowing people to encrypt and decrypt data. Can be used to perform other mathematical operations as well. Given a cipher, a key determines the mapping of the plaintext to the ciphertext. The types of keys include distributed key, private key, public key, secret key, session key, shared key, subkey, symmetric key, and weak key.
A method of encrypting key data for protection on untrusted storage devices or during transmission over an insecure channel.
Message Authentication Code. Apiece of information is attached to a message for verification of the messages authenticity and data integrity.
A message digest algorithm, which hashes an arbitrary-length input into a 16-byte digest. Designed as a replacement for MD4.
National Institute of Standards and Technology. A division of the US Department of Commerce (formerly known as the NBS) which produces security and cryptography-related standards.
Optimal Asymmetric Encryption Padding. A padding scheme often used with RSA encryption to convert the deterministic RSA encryption scheme into a probabilistic scheme.
Output Feedback. A mode of encryption in which the cipher is decoupled from its ciphertext.
A function that uses the HMAC-HASH as the core function in its construction. Specified in RFC 2246 and RFC 5246.
Password-based Key Derivation Function 2. A method of password-based key derivation, which applies a MAC algorithm to derive the key.
Public Key Cryptography Standards. A group of public-key cryptography standards devised and published by RSA®.
Pseudo Random Function. A function that can be used to generate output from a random seed and a data variable such that the output is computationally indistinguishable from truly random output.
The state or quality of being secluded from the view or presence of others.
The secret key in public key cryptography. Primarily used for decryption but also used for encryption with digital signatures.
Public Security Parameters. Security related public information, for example, public keys, whose modification can compromise the security of the cryptographic module.
Probabilistic Signature Scheme. A cryptographic signature scheme typically used with RSA signatures.

Public key, asymmetric, algorithm providing the ability to encrypt data and create and verify digital signatures. RSA stands for Rivest, Shamir, and Adleman, the developers of the RSA public key cryptosystem.
Secure Hash Algorithm. An algorithm which creates a unique hash value for each possible input. SHA takes an arbitrary input which is hashed into a 160-bit digest.
A revision to SHA to correct a weakness. It produces 160-bit digests. SHA-1 takes an arbitrary input, which is hashed into a 20-byte digest.

The NIST-mandated successor to SHA-1, to complement the Advanced Encryption Standard. It is a family of message digest algorithms (SHA2-224, SHA2-256, SHA2-384, SHA2-512, SHA2-512/224, and SHA2-512/256), which produce digests of 224, 256, 384, 512, 224, and 256 bits respectively.
A family of hash algorithms which includes SHA3-224,SHA3-256, SHA3-384 and SHA3-512 bits. It is an alternative to SHA-2,as no significant attacks on SHA-2 are currently known.
An extendable output function (XOF) that produces a variable digest size.
Secure Shell Key Derivation Function. Used by the SSH protocol to derive IVs, encryption keys and integrity keys.
Sensitive Security Parameters include both Critical Security Parameters (CSP) and Public Security Parameters (PSP).
XEX-based Tweaked Codebook mode with ciphertext stealing. A mode of encryption used with AES.
